content: Most abdominal and pelvic incidental findings are {{c1::benign findings detected on imaging obtained for unrelated indications}}.
A major goal of incidental finding management is to avoid {{c1::unnecessary follow-up, procedures, anxiety, cost, and cascades of care}}.
In low-risk patients, small homogeneous flash-filling liver lesions at CT generally {{c1::do not require follow-up}}.
Homogeneously hyperenhancing liver lesions larger than 1.5 cm or heterogeneously enhancing lesions at single-phase CECT warrant {{c1::follow-up MRI}}.
Classic hyperechoic liver lesions at US in low-risk patients are highly likely {{c1::hemangiomas}}.
Asymptomatic biliary ductal dilatation at CECT with no obstructing cause and normal liver function tests generally {{c1::does not require additional follow-up}}.
A homogeneous renal mass measuring 21-30 HU at portal venous phase CECT is most likely {{c1::a benign hyperattenuating cyst or pseudoenhancing cyst}}.
Small bowel intussusceptions 3.5 cm or longer, with obstruction or wall thickening, are higher risk for {{c1::an underlying lead point}}.
Misty mesentery without lymphadenopathy or malignancy history generally {{c1::does not require further follow-up}}.
In low-risk patients, testicular microlithiasis {{c1::does not warrant follow-up imaging}}.
